# Gender Dynamics in Collaborative Settings: 

# Analyzing Communication Variation in Same-Gender and 

# Mixed-Gender Task Completion 

> Submitted to
> Professor Jeffrey Flory
> by
> Bella C. Lynch
> for
> Senior Thesis
> Fall 2024
> 2 December 2024 2 Lynch

Abstract 

Previous research has highlighted the implications of pervasive gender-based stereotypes 

in workplace environments. Women may face barriers to entry and advancement compared to 

their male counterparts, and can be undermined in assessments of competency and skill. 

Unconscious biases, shaped by societal norms and cognitive distortions, may perpetuate 

inequities and influence how workers communicate and collaborate, particularly in team settings. 

This thesis investigates the complex dynamics of workplace communication and behavioral 

patterns across varying gender pair compositions, using collaborative task completion as the 

focal point. I analyze transcriptions of work-team conversations derived from a pilot for a large 

natural field experiment and find significant differences in gender-based communication 

strategies. Key findings indicate that the presence of males in team settings increases the 

frequency of decision-switching and partner interruptions. These insights support existing 

gender-based communication theories, demonstrating that gender composition in collaborative 

task completion significantly influences both group effectiveness and the overall dynamics. This 

research underscores the importance of fostering more diverse and inclusive workplace 

environments to mitigate gender discrimination. 

Keywords: gender-based stereotypes, cognitive biases, communication, switching-decision, 

interrupting partner 3 Lynch 

Table of Contents 

I. Introduction.................................................................................................................................. 4 

II. Literature Review....................................................................................................................... 7 

III. Data Overview & Experimental Design..................................................................................12 

IV. Procedure................................................................................................................................. 16 

V. Results....................................................................................................................................... 19 

VI. Results Discussion...................................................................................................................21 

VII. Conclusion............................................................................................................................. 28 

VIII. References............................................................................................................................ 31 

IX. Appendix................................................................................................................................. 33 4 Lynch 

I. Introduction 

Gender differences in communication may play a pivotal role in workplace dynamics, 

consequently impacting team performance and task effectiveness. Understanding the underlying 

biases and societal pressures that influence these behavioral variations is essential for fostering 

more inclusive workplaces. Evidence suggests that United States firms posit gender imbalances 

and disparities in representation, raising important questions for further scientific inquiry. Ninety 

percent of Fortune 500 CEOs are white males, while only six percent are women, and fewer than 

four percent are African American or Hispanic (Flory et al., 2021). Despite growing calls for 

change, management demographics have remained largely stagnant since the early 2000s, 

prompting a new generation of workers to demand reform and place innumerous pressures on 

firms and organizations spanning Corporate America. Firms have responded accordingly by 

investing copious amounts of capital into diversity initiatives, spending an estimated $10 billion 

annually on training and up to $200 million on firm-specific programs (Flory et al., 2021). 

While diversity is a multi-dimensional concept, gender remains a particularly salient 

factor. Women, specifically, often face disadvantages in the workplace, fueled by gender 

stereotypes that diminish their perceived capabilities and hinder their professional growth. 

Physiological gender differences may contribute to this disparity, as research suggests men often 

display higher confidence, greater competitiveness, and lower aversion to risk – traits associated 

with workplace success (Niederle & Vesterlund, 2007). Other explanations for the persistence of 

rigid gender inequities stem from societal framework and long-standing associations portraying 

females as emotional and reserved (Furomo & Pearson, 2007). These biases have historically 

marginalized women, particularly in team settings, undervaluing their contributions and 

subsequently impeding their career progression. 5 Lynch 

Contrary to the traditional perspective that views gender differences as limitations, recent 

research instead challenges and emphasizes their potential as strategic advantages. In fact, 

mixed-gender teams often demonstrate improved cohesion, effectiveness, and innovation by 

promoting diverse perspectives. Woolley et al. (2010) shows that teams with more women tend 

to have higher collective intelligence, in part due to women’s greater interpersonal awareness, 

which enhances collaborative performance. The interplay between communication styles – 

women’s prioritization of emotional awareness paired with men’s assertiveness – can create a 

balanced dynamic that drives optimal performance and fosters inclusion (Woolley et al., 2010). 

This empirical evidence underscores the value of diversity in team collaborative task completion 

and challenges stereotypes that limit women’s contributions and potential. 

This thesis explores the intersection of gender composition and communication styles in 

collaborative task completion, analyzing variations across Male-Male, Female-Female, and 

Male-Female teams. Leveraging transcriptions derived from a recent pilot dataset for a new 

experiment on worker identity and workplace communication, I identified ten key variables for 

analysis: (1) Coordinated Decision-Making, (2) Seeking Agreement / Common-Ground, (3) 

Humor / Sarcasm, (4) Self-Deprecation, (5) Sentence Fragmentation, (6) Interrupting Partner, (7) 

Interpersonal Conversation, (8) Frustration, (9) Switching Decision (to match partner), and (10) 

Uncertainty (surrounding task completion). Although the dataset’s limited scale precludes broad 

generalizations, preliminary analyses reveal notable patterns in communication differences. 

Findings indicate that the presence of males is correlated with increased probability of 

decision-switching, with statistically significant results ( ). Additionally, the presence 𝑝 < 0. 01 

of females in teams is associated with reduced probability of partner interruptions, though this 6 Lynch 

result only approaches significance . Overall, these trends are influential and (𝑝 = 0. 27) 

intriguing, warranting further investigation to explore the underlying causes. 

By analyzing communication dynamics in same-gender and mixed-gender teams, this 

thesis contributes to the broader literature and empirical research investigating gender diversity 

and team behaviors. Specifically, this research highlights how gendered communication styles 

influence decision-making, connection, and conflict resolution in group settings. These insights 

may prove useful for guiding firms in developing more inclusive and effective teams. Ultimately, 

this thesis advocates for moving beyond traditional gender stereotypes to cultivate inclusive 

workplace cultures. By leveraging communication differences as strengths rather than barriers, 

firms can drive creativity, efficiency, and long-term success. 7 Lynch 

II. Literature Review 

This section examines the scientifically established effects of gender composition on 

team dynamics and work quality. Specifically, this literature review clarifies what is known 

regarding team productivity and outcomes while highlighting gaps in research related to 

gender-based communication. First, prominent social frameworks that underlie economic 

theories regarding individual behavior and subsequent contributions in group settings will be 

introduced. These sociological theories offer insights into communication differences between 

genders and offer a preliminary introduction to the research question at hand. After establishing 

the theoretical framework, empirical evidence regarding gender, contribution levels, and team 

performance will be presented. The review will then isolate communication as a key variable of 

interest, analyzing its mediating role in the relationship between gender diversity and 

performance outcomes. While this literature review draws on a variety of highly credited studies, 

much still remains to be learned about the defined research question. Accordingly, this section 

not only synthesizes existing research but also identifies critical gaps that this thesis aims to 

address. 

Theoretical Background 

Previous research to date has extensively examined how societal norms and expectations 

influence gender-based leadership and group communication. Studies consistently use 

psychological and social theories to guide research questions and explain behavioral differences 

between genders. A prominent framework in this area includes role and structural theory, which 

posits that men and women are expected to fulfill distinct social roles both within and outside the 

workplace (Schiller et al., 2011). Within these theories, women are characterized as 8 Lynch 

non-confrontational and avoidant of antagonistic settings, instead displaying prioritization of 

cohesion and high levels of socio-emotional awareness (Andrews, 1992). Men, conversely, are 

perceived as competitive and increasingly dominant in nature, often encouraging conflict to drive 

performance (Furumo & Pearson, 2007). These gender-based attributes, rooted in theoretical 

frameworks, shape how genders perceive each other’s roles, reinforcing stereotypical behaviors 

and biases in group dynamics and communication (Schiller, et al., 2011). Such structures, which 

serve as foundational pillars perpetuating gender stereotypes, are frequently used by the 

scientific community to explore inherent differences in men’s and women’s behaviors. In the 

context of this thesis, role and structural theories provide an essential lens for approaching the 

research question. 

Empirical Evidence 

Having outlined the theoretical foundations that shape gender-based societal norms, this 

section presents specific empirical findings that reveal the impact of gender diversity on team 

performance and outcomes. Numerous studies suggest that increased gender diversity within 

teams is correlated with higher performance outcomes (Sunder et al., 2024). Yang et al. (2022), 

for example, examined the effects of gender composition on routine establishment and 

adaptability. The researchers found that gender-diverse teams developed routine and stability 

more efficiently and demonstrated greater adaptability to external changes compared to 

gender-homogeneous teams. These findings reinforce the notion that gender-heterogeneity is 

essential for achieving optimal performance (Yang et al., 2022). Similarly, Maddi and Gingras 

(2012) identified positive correlations between gender diversity and both the quantity and quality 

of work output. Hoogendoorn, et al. (2013) furthers this, emphasizing that gender-balanced 

teams significantly outperform gender-homogenous teams in decision-making and financial 9 Lynch 

results. These empirical findings underscore the significant positive correlation between gender 

diversity and team performance. Ultimately, diversity fosters a unique array of perspectives 

contributing to critical thinking, problem-solving, and creativity, which collectively enhances 

team performance. 

Communication as a Mediating Factor 

While previous research has established the positive effects of gender diversity on team 

outcomes, few studies have isolated communication as a mediating variable of analysis. This 

thesis focuses on the role of communication – specifically styles of information exchange, 

conflict resolution, and coordination – and its impact on team performance. Drawing from 

structural and role theory, it is well-documented that men and women employ distinct 

communication styles shaped by societal expectations and perceived roles (Karpowitz et al., 

2024). Guiller & Dundell’s (2006) study on online education discussions support this notion, 

with results revealing that women tend to use more attenuated language, frequently employing 

soft-spoken adjectives and exhibiting high levels of agreeableness. In contrast, men are more 

direct, unafraid to voice explicit opinions and increasingly willing to engage in confrontational 

discussions (Sussman & Tyson, 2000). The interplay between these differing communication 

styles offers a rich area for analysis, specifically regarding its impact on team cohesion and 

decision-making processes. Women’s emphasis on trust and collaboration may enhance 

efficiency and effectiveness, while men’s forthright style fosters diversity of thought (Sunder et 

al., 2024; Prinsen et al., 2007). Furthering this, Woolley et al. (2010) argue that women’s 

contributions raise a “collective intelligence” factor within group communication, leading to 

optimal performance. Conversely, men’s assertive tendencies can spark innovation and challenge 

contrasting viewpoints. Together, these stylistic differences emphasize the potential advantages 10 Lynch 

of gender-diverse team communication dynamics, driving increased innovation, productivity, and 

cohesion. 

Limitations of Existing Research 

While previous empirical research offers valuable insights into the impact of 

gender-diverse team dynamics on outcomes, not all studies support the notion that varied team 

composition consistently enhances overall performance (Apesteguia et al., 2012). In fact, some 

studies report that gender-diverse teams do not outperform gender-homogenous teams in 

significant ways (Lee & Farh, 2004). Moreover, gender diversity may result in tension-filled 

dynamics, suggesting that: “diversity is a double-edged sword in contemporary organizational 

theory,” (Horwitz & Howitz, 2007). Evidence further suggests that male team members 

frequently exclude or marginalize female influence and seldom relinquish leadership positions 

(Karpowitz et al., 2024). These findings reinforce the complexities of team diversity, 

highlighting that existing gender stereotypes may influence group dynamics and lead to 

non-optimal results. 

Additionally, many studies examining gendered communication styles and their impact 

on team performance face challenges with internal validity. Hoogendoorn et al. (2013) note that: 

“studies based on observational data are likely to be plagued by various endogeneity issues, 

including reversed causality, unobserved heterogeneity, and (self-)selection into and out of 

teams.” These challenges complicate researchers’ ability to separate the direct effects of gender 

diversity on team performance, especially when incorporating communication as an additional 

variable. 11 Lynch 

This thesis addresses these limitations by isolating communication as the central variable 

of interest, analyzing its role as a mediator or moderator between gender diversity and team 

performance. By focusing specifically on communication, this research seeks to deepen existing 

knowledge and uncover nuanced insights into the interplay between gender and communication 

dynamics. While research suggests that increased inclusivity in group settings may lead to 

optimal outcomes, this notion remains an important area for exploration to fully understand its 

impact on team performance and the persistent gender gap. 12 Lynch 

III. Data Overview & Experimental Design 

Sourcing Data 

To analyze the research question at hand, data was leveraged from a pilot set for a natural 

field experiment that will be conducted to simulate real-world team environments by researchers 

Jeffrey Flory, John List, Amanda Pallais, and Brent Hickman. Sourcing this data from 

researchers with similar motivations provided relevant context and insights into team dynamics 

and communication in work environments. The data used for this analysis comes from a phase 

where student workers were paid to participate, unaware that they were part of an experiment. 

For the larger-scale dataset to be collected in the future, the experiment will involve the 

recruitment of job seekers interested in data entry roles for the consultancy firm, FHiLiPS Data 

Analytics, LLC. 400 data-entry workers will be hired for a short-term period of three weeks, with 

researchers defining the ultimate goal as analyzing individuals and teams production efforts and 

behaviors. The data entry task will be completed remotely using the professional web-based 

platform, DataMade, which will provide several advantages for conducting a realistic natural 

field experiment. Firstly, the web platform will allow the accumulation of a sample population 

that is widely representative of the U.S. labor market. Secondly, researchers will be able to assess 

the effects of differing work environments – solo, team, and peer-review – facilitating 

straightforward analysis. Finally, the platform will grant researchers detailed insights through 

time-stamped conversations, audio/video recordings, and surveys. These features will enable 

researchers to capture authentic workplace environments and worker interactions while ensuring 

adherence to the criteria of natural field experiments. 

Classification as a Natural Field Experiment 13 Lynch 

Flory, List, Pallais, and Hickman’s experimental design allows for establishment of 

internal and external validity in data collection by aligning with the SEAP (Subject, 

Environment, Awareness, Participation) framework that is frequently utilized in experimental 

economics. For subjects (S), participants will remain unaware of their involvement in the 

experiment, simply applying for a short-term data entry job with compensation. By doing so, 

Flory, List, Pallais, and Hickman will accumulate a diverse subject pool, consisting of 

individuals from the U.S. labor force with a genuine interest in the task at hand, thereby ensuring 

the validity of the results. For environment (E), the data collection process will take place in a 

real-world setting. Although workers will collaborate on a web platform, the environment will be 

natural, and tasks will be completed as usual, without participant awareness of the study. For 

awareness (A), participants will again be unaware that their interactions and behaviors are being 

analyzed, allowing researchers to observe genuine task completion. Finally, participation (P) will 

be passive, as though participants will express interest in the task on a compensation basis, they 

will not give explicit consent to participate in an experiment. The SEAP framework is critical for 

ensuring the validity of results and making conclusive statements in field experiments. Since 

Flory, List, Pallais, and Hickman will meet the SEAP criteria for their natural field experiment, 

including the pilot set used in this thesis, the use of their data for analyzing gender differences in 

communication styles is justified and ensures the reliability of the insights obtained. Lastly it is 

important to note that this design provides far greater external validity than most studies in the 

field to date, given its real-world context described above. 

Experimental Design Overview 

Flory, List, Pallais, and Hickman will ensure robustness of results by incorporating a 

two-stage randomization process. Randomization reduces biases, ensuring that each participant 14 Lynch 

has an equivalent chance of being placed in the group of interest (treatment group) for analysis. 

By minimizing selection bias, experimenters will ensure groups are attributable to both the 

sample of interest and population at large. Randomization additionally allows for confidence in 

inferring causation when examining differences in behavior and results across groups, making it 

easier to draw conclusions. Lastly, randomization provides an element of generalizability, which 

will allow for researchers to scale-up in their findings from the observed sample to the greater 

population at large. This incorporation of randomization is thus a critical pillar contributing to 

both the internal and external validity of the experiment. 

Although the larger-scale experiment has yet to occur, it is important to highlight the 

systematic design for data collection that will be implemented. Flory, List, Pallais, and Hickman 

will follow a clear framework for recruiting workers, which involves posting online 

advertisements across multiple U.S. cities on job boards and inviting individuals to apply for 

data-entry roles. The advertisements will detail the firm’s hiring needs and provide a link for 

further information. Upon clicking the link, individuals will be randomly assigned to treatment 

and control groups. This experiment will be conducted entirely online utilizing the service 

defined above, DataMade. This platform offers a video-chat window, where participants can see 

each other and communicate. Researchers will be provided access to detailed timestamped 

responses including session durations, specific work times, final submissions, and all audio and 

video recordings. Participants will engage in tasks alternating between solo work, teamwork, and 

peer-review settings. Transcriptions of the collected data will be processed using Amazon Web 

Services, ensuring detailed documentation of interactions. The data entry task will involve visual 

information processing, requiring real-time judgment and collaboration. 

Pilot Set for This Thesis 15 Lynch 

Transitioning now to the explicit pilot set being leveraged for this thesis, a highly similar 

experimental design was conducted. The subjects in this pilot set were recruited from the 

Claremont Colleges (the 5Cs) campuses through posters, with participants being paid $19 per 

hour to work for a 2-hour block from 8am to 10pm on four different days. The same platform, 

DataMade, and experimental processes were employed during the pilot. In each session, 

participants were asked to code Google Street View images according to 16 quantitative and 

qualitative variables, such as counting visible signs or assessing street quality. In each session, 

participants coded five images in joint teams of two. Both team members worked on their 

individual screens but were required to reconcile any differing responses on a resolution page 

before moving on to the next image. This pilot set forms the foundation for the analysis in this 

thesis, providing valuable insights into team dynamics and communication styles, while aligning 

with the methodology of the larger-scale experiment to ensure both internal and external validity. 16 Lynch 

IV. Procedure 

As demonstrated above, the pilot data collected is both valid and generalizable, making it 

highly relevant for addressing the central research question of this thesis: how gender 

composition influences team behaviors and dynamics, particularly focusing on communication 

style. Flory, List, Pallais, and Hickman’s experimental design effectively captures variations in 

collaboration and work productivity across different gender and racial groups, providing a strong 

foundation for exploring the nuances of gender-based communication styles and their workplace 

implications. 

Upon receiving the preliminary data gathered by researchers, I began my analysis by 

uploading all recordings captured through the online web platform to Amazon Web Services 

(AWS). AWS tracked and transcribed each file in the dataset, for a total sample size of 65 

recordings. Upon cleaning the transcriptions and excluding beta test and non-usable files, the 

total number of recordings was reduced to 35. Once the dataset was finalized and each transcript 

was assigned a unique, identifiable name, I initiated my preliminary investigation into the 

research question at hand. 

To identify common characteristics across the files, I utilized AI software (ChatGPT) to 

conduct an initial analysis, employing the specific command: “Please analyze the following 

transcript for communication style between individuals who are engaging in a group task.” 

ChatGPT identified eight to ten key themes present within each transcript, ultimately enabling 

me to detect overlaps and commonalities across the dataset. 

From this initial scan, I identified ten key variables of interest that were critical for 

analyzing differences in communication style by gender. These variables included: (1) 

Coordinated Decision-Making, (2) Seeking Agreement / Common-Ground, (3) Humor / 17 Lynch 

Sarcasm, (4) Self-Deprecation, (5) Sentence Fragmentation, (6) Interrupting Partner, (7) 

Interpersonal Conversation, (8) Frustration, (9) Switching Decision (to match partner), and (10) 

Uncertainty (surrounding task completion). I proceeded to conduct my own investigation, 

manually searching all 35 transcripts for the presence of the ten variables and accordingly 

assigning binary values. An assignment of 1 indicated the presence of the variable within the 

respective transcript, whereas an assignment 0 indicated its absence. It is important to note that 

the assignment of these binary indicators for the ten variables of interest within each transcript is 

subject to both ChatGPT’s analysis and my own interpretation of the conversations. 

Finally, each transcript was assigned a gender identity indicator based on the composition 

of the team: MM (Male-Male teams), FF (Female-Female teams), and MF (Male-Female teams). 

Similarly to the variables of interest, each transcript was assigned a binary value of 0 or 1 based 

on its respective gender composition. This assignment was conducted last to minimize any 

potential unconscious biases surrounding gender composition that might have influenced AI’s or 

my interpretations of the transcripts when probing for the presence of the ten variables of 

interest. The following table summarizes the binary variable assignment for each transcript:

# 3 ways diversity can impact team dynamics

### The article at a glance

Three recent studies by new Cambridge Judge faculty members Elizabeth George and Prithviraj Chattopadhyay look at how diversity in gender, geography and pay grade affect the modern workplace.

Category: 
[Equality, diversity and inclusion](/category/equality-diversity-inclusion)
[Insight](/category/insight)
[Leadership and organisational behaviour](/category/leadership-organisational-behaviour)

Two of the newest members of the Cambridge Judge Business School faculty focus their research on the workplace, examining issues ranging from non-standard work (such as the gig economy) to diversity within teams in order to better understand how the modern working world operates.

Elizabeth George, KPMG Professor of Management Studies, and Prithviraj Chattopadhyay, Professor of Organisational Behaviour, arrived in Cambridge in autumn 2023 after an 11,000-mile journey from Auckland, New Zealand, following 7 years at the University of Auckland Business School. Their previous train stops have included studies in India and in Austin, Texas (where both earned their PhDs), and academic posts in Hong Kong, Sydney, Brisbane, and Michigan.

Their research has featured in some of the leading business and psychology journals in the world, including the Academy of Management Journal, Administrative Science Quarterly, Organization Science, and Journal of Applied Psychology.

![Elizabeth George.](https://www.jbs.cam.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/elizabeth-george-703x396-1.jpg)

Professor Elizabeth George

![Prithviraj Chattopadhyay.](https://www.jbs.cam.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/prithviraj-chattopadhyay-703x396-1.jpg)

## 3 studies focus on diversity in teams in the workplace

To introduce our readers to Elizabeth and Prithviraj, we focus in this article on 3 studies in recent years they conducted jointly or individually that focus on important issues of the workplace:

Although the studies are very different, they all have a common theme: how do people of diverse characteristics – be it gender, location/origin, or status – best function in an organisation to achieve positive organisational results? “Underlining all 3 studies is the idea that individuals use demographic attributes to sort themselves and their teammates into in-groups versus out-groups that are assigned high versus low status,” says Prithviraj. “People wish to enhance their sense of self through either competing for status or collaborating with high-status colleagues, depending on what is seen to be possible within the context of the status hierarchy governing their interactions.

“We were drawn to these topics because we have observed so many examples of groups and individuals who thrive in conditions of dissimilarity and those that don’t. We were curious about why they had these different outcomes”, adds Elizabeth”.

People wish to enhance their sense of self through either competing for status or collaborating with high-status colleagues, depending on what is seen to be possible within the context of the status hierarchy governing their interactions

### 1. The impact of first impressions in geographically diverse teams

It’s a fact of modern workplace life that people work in geographically diverse teams – diverse in that people are physically located in many parts of the world in today’s Zoom-enabled offices and homes, and diverse in that people working in the same physical office may come from all parts of a nation or from around the globe.

So how do people influence and interact in such geographically varied teams?

A study co-authored by Prithviraj and Elizabeth in the Academy of Management Journal focuses on the emotions experienced by team members in their very first meetings, and how these emotions influence the effect of geographical dissimilarities over time.

In 2 studies based among MBA students in Hong Kong and India, the paper finds that among individuals who experience pleasant emotions in initial meetings the geographical dissimilarity with colleagues is more positively related with perceived influence in the initial stage of a project as they tend to create a positive impression from the beginning. But for those experiencing unpleasant emotions such as sadness in first impressions, such geographic dissimilarity is instead related with perceived influence in later stages of a project. Sadness signals to individuals that there is a problem to solve, leading them to gain influence over time.

As for those experiencing unpleasant emotions such as anger in initial meetings, the study found that it was more of a roller-coaster: “Their geographic dissimilarity was initially positively and later negatively related with perceived influence”, the paper finds, attributing the initial positive effect to the energy generated by anger.

#### Exploring how team motivation is shaped by feelings and distance

The practical implications of these findings are that for organisations to realise the benefits of diversity for team performance it is important to understand these consequences, because the emotions experienced and level of geographical dissimilarity can shape the motivation of team members and their ability to work effectively with colleagues and thus be perceived as influential by them.

“Our results show that the geographical dissimilarity of teammates could either facilitate or hinder the emergence of team member influence at different points in a project, depending on those team members’ initial emotional experiences,” the study finds. Due to the tendency of individuals to remain consistent in their view over time, their initial pattern of interactions with dissimilar colleagues endures over the life of a project.

Business schools were chosen as the study’s setting because they are places that bring together people from around the world to learn and network together. The first study sample totalled 192 people belonging to 27 countries led in numbers by China, the US, Canada, and India, with a range of full-time employment of 2 to 14 years. The second study included 338 people in 75 teams of 5 to 7 members from 23 states in India associated with differences in language or dialect and salient aspects of their culture.

The study in the Academy of Management Journal – entitled “Geographical dissimilarity and team member influence: do emotions experienced in the initial team meeting matter?” – is co-authored by Prithviraj Chattopadhyay and Elizabeth George, now at Cambridge Judge Business School; Jiping Li of Frankfurt School of Finance & Management, and Vishal Gupta of the Indian Institute of Management in Ahmedabad, India.

Our results show that the geographical dissimilarity of teammates could either facilitate or hinder the emergence of team member influence at different points in a project, depending on those team members’ initial emotional experiences.

### 2. Males unwilling to accept female leaders can shape team dynamics

Research over many years has suggested that male subordinates are less willing to accept female leaders compared to female subordinates – but how does this shape team dynamics involving other members of the team?

A study co-authored by Prithviraj in the Journal of Applied Social Psychology finds that males who are unwilling to accept a female leader may seek to shape the group dynamic by inviting other male team members to develop their own influence within the team.

Such men may also seek to increase their own intra-team influence through ‘competence monitoring’ (behaviour to boost their own influence), and especially when there is a greater proportion of male peers in the team. That’s “because men (more than women) are likely to disengage from the team, rather than actively seeking influence within the team, when there are fewer same‐gender peers in the team,” the study says.

#### What is the impact of gender dissimilarity between team members?

The research differs from previous studies in that it focuses not only on the gender of the team leader but also to team members’ gender dissimilarity to the leader, so this allows the study “to explore the individual‐level behaviours and outcomes of subordinates” – not in isolation but instead in the context of other team members with the same supervisor.

The research is based on a survey of 288 members of 61 research and development teams (R&D teams were chosen because they work closely together toward a common goal) at 11 high-tech companies in Beijing, 7 of them in the biopharmaceutical field, 2 in electronics and 2 in transportation. Male team members and male leaders accounted for 55.2% and 72.1% of the sample, respectively, and the average age was 28.28 years for team members and 33.36 years for team leaders.

In summary: “Our findings suggest that individual team members’ motivation to use competence monitoring to gain influence in their team is shaped by the combination of their gender, the team leader’s gender, and their peers’ gender. Awareness of these dynamics can be useful when designing a team.”

The study in the Journal of Applied Social Psychology – entitled “Team members’ reactions to a gender-dissimilar leader: competence monitoring and influence” – was co-authored by Jiping Li of the Frankfurt School of Finance & Management; Daan van Knippenberg of Rice University in Houston, Texas; Prithviraj Chattopadhyay, now at Cambridge Judge Business School; and Wen Wu of Beijing Jiaotong University.

Our findings suggest that individual team members' motivation to use competence monitoring to gain influence in their team is shaped by the combination of their gender, the team leader's gender, and their peers' gender.

### 3. Legitimate pay discrepancy can boost team identification and performance

Pay discrepancy within organisations and within teams can breed envy and resentment, and this reflects human nature. Many studies have looked at this issue from a broad organisational perspective, but research by Elizabeth and Prithviraj takes a more nuanced approach focused on individuals and their immediate teams.

The findings: working with teammates with a different pay grade can have beneficial effects on individuals by reducing social uncertainty in their work relationships – and this can boost their team identification and performance while lowering their turnover intention, particularly for team members in a lower pay grade. But there is a big caveat: this works only when team members perceive the pay discrepancy to be legitimate and stable.

This is more the case when considering pay grade rather than pay itself, “because higher grades are associated with higher education and skills that presumably result in greater value for the organisation… or are associated with better performance on the part of the individual… Some combination of education, skill and performance form the basis of determining pay grades in most organisations.”

Most research on pay discrepancy has been conducted at collective levels (company-wide or workforce-wide) to look at conditions under which pay dispersion is beneficial or harmful to the organisation, but the study co-authored by Elizabeth and Prithviraj (published in the Academy of Management Journal) instead examines how pay difference affects people through a process termed “uncertainty reduction”.

#### Why pay grade disparities need to be studied within team rather than across organisation

“We focus particularly on pay grade differences between teammates because teams form the building blocks of today’s organisations, and interactions with teammates, more so than with colleagues in the broader organisation, are salient features of the everyday life of team members that shape their social self-concept,” the study says.

The research is based on members and leaders of 40 marketing teams from a telecommunications company in China that develop marketing plans, provide customer services and manage customer relationships. Within each team are jobs with associated pay grades based on the worth of those jobs to the company, and team members with greater skills and knowledge are recruited for jobs with more responsibility.

The study says it is “the first to suggest that working with dissimilar teammates may lead to reduced uncertainty regarding issues like who provides leadership who is more a follower, and the social pecking order in the team, and thereby facilitate identification with the team. Our study demonstrates that people can embrace differences in teams and realise the informational benefits related to dissimilarity when they perceive that status differences related to dissimilarity are legitimate and stable.”

The study in the Academy of Management Journal – entitled “Not in my pay grade: the relational benefit of pay grade dissimilarity” – is co-authored by Lida L. Zhang of the University of Macau and by Elizabeth George and Prithviraj Chattopadhyay, now of Cambridge Judge Business School.

We focus particularly on pay grade differences between teammates because teams form the building blocks of today’s organisations, and interactions with teammates, more so than with colleagues in the broader organisation, are salient features of the everyday life of team members that shape their social self-concept

## Breadcrumbs

## Breadcrumb

# How Structure and Gender Composition Effect A Team’s Collective Ability to Get Work Done

Leaders seeking to foster inclusivity should be aware that a team’s collective intelligence—its general ability to make decisions, innovate, plan, and more—is significantly influenced by its hierarchal structure and also by its gender composition.

**The Center for Innovative Leadership (CIL)** exists to support and accelerate the production of new research on leadership in today’s dynamic, complex organizations and bring research to life through engaging student experiences and powerful industry partnerships.

**Summary**

A team’s general ability to perform across a wide range of tasks—from creative endeavors to complex decision making to planning and execution—is known as its ‘collective intelligence.’ One key factor predicting a team’s collective intelligence, and thus likely effectiveness, is the team’s ‘collective attention,’ or the extent to which team members are jointly attending to the task and each other.

A recent study has investigated how a team’s structure, particularly its hierarchy, leads to different displays of collective attention—evidenced through different speaking patterns. The authors found that stable hierarchies increased cooperative, harmonious speaking patterns; whereas teams with unstable hierarchies, where a leader could be unseated, exhibited more competitive, interruptive speaking patterns. But both, it turns out, could reflect collective attention depending on group norms. For majority female groups, more cooperative speaking patterns captured their collective attention and predicted their collective intelligence; but in all-male groups, it was the more competitive pattern of speaking that indicated collective attention and predicted intelligence. Understanding these dynamics—the influence of a team’s hierarchy and its composition—can help organizations design teams and establish effective norms to support collective intelligence and performance within an inclusive environment.

Teams and teamwork are fundamental to how organizations operate. Developments over recent years—flatter management structures, remote working, efforts towards diversity and inclusion—have added to the complexity for leaders looking to maximize team performance, calling for a better understanding of the drivers of team success in these new environments.  
  
**Collective Intelligence and Collective Attention**

A principal factor in a team’s performance is its ‘ collective intelligence’ , a term used in biology to describe the behavior of bacteria and animals, and now also with humans. In humans, collective intelligence specifically refers to a team's general ability to work together and perform well on various tasks, from creative endeavors to complex decision making to planning and execution. Distinct from simply how talented or smart the individuals are, collective intelligence captures a team’s ability to be greater than the sum of its parts.

Previous research has shown that ‘collective attention’ along with two other cognitive functions—collective memory and collective reasoning—can explain why one team might have higher collective intelligence than another. A team’s level of collective attention refers to the quality and coordination of members’ focus. Collective attention is characterized by members’ joint attention to each other and to the task, thus increasing the combined thinking power focused on the coordinated effort required to accomplish team goals. At a time when the abundance of information brought through digitization along with seemingly endless distractions have taxed people’s personal attention span, it is especially important to seek ways to better manage attention in a collective, team context.  
  
**Competitive and Cooperative Team Dynamics**

The way a team operates is established very early on in its existence, determined by the team’s composition, or traits of team members, and the team’s structure, such as its hierarchy. And while it is not always possible to choose a team’s members or to arrange its hierarchy, it is important that leaders are aware of these dynamics when creating and managing teams so that the current conditions can best be leveraged.   
  
In a new study, Anna Mayo, an affiliate of the Center for Innovative Leadership at Johns Hopkins Business School, looked closely first at how team hierarchy influences collective attention and consequently collective intelligence, and secondly at the additional effect of the gender composition of team membership.

Mayo and colleagues randomly assigned 150 teams to work in stable, unstable, or unspecified hierarchical structures and various gender compositions, and they recorded different behavioral manifestations of collective attention as evidenced in each team’s speaking patterns.   
  
The type of hierarchy  a team has is part of its foundational structure. A hierarchy provides team members with a guide to knowing who does what, when, and how, who takes the lead and who to defer to—expectations that have implications for collective attention. Yet, while many have looked at the type of leader or the steepness of the hierarchy, this study looked specifically at the effect of the ‘stability’ of the hierarchy.

Research findings suggested that the stable hierarchies facilitated cooperation such that team members interacted harmoniously, speaking in a more synchronous pattern. In contrast, when the hierarchy was unstable or non-existent, such that an acknowledged leader—formal or informal—might lose that position, the research findings suggested that team members were more likely to engage in competitive, interruptive behaviors.

One might assume harmonious spoken interaction to be the best gauge of collective attention, but it is not the only way. In group interaction, conversational interruptions, where team members talk over one another, can also be an indicator of high levels of collective attention and engagement in certain circumstances. Neuroimaging research has even shown that brain regions associated with mind activation are recruited to a greater extent when individuals are in competitive contexts than in cooperative ones. Team structure can thus influence collective attention through encouraging harmonious cooperative interactions, but also through the opposite, with competitive and interruptive interactions.

The impact of these patterns of interaction on collective intelligence, then, might not be straightforward. Rather collective intelligence will be dependent on whether the team members are individuals who respond better to either cooperative or competitive situations.

**Nuances of Team Composition**

This brings us to the composition of team membership and specifically to gender composition. Given a variety of socialization processes, majority female teams are typically disposed towards equal participation and interactional synchronicity. Women, relative to men, tend to avoid competitive situations and potential conflict. And in female dominated teams, even male members tend to engage in cooperative behavior. In such groups, an egalitarian and synchronous pattern of interaction is likely consistent with expectations and can signal that everyone is attending to the task *and* each other. Men on the other hand are commonly socialized to be more assertive and dominant; they are less uncomfortable with conflict and might both select into and thrive in competitive environments. Here too, in male dominated teams all members, including women, tend to engage in more competitive behaviors. Thus, in these teams, competition is expected and could be a signal of greater attention and effort being applied to a task. Indeed, the research findings suggest that in majority female teams, it was synchronicity that indicated more collective attention and was predictive of the team’s collective intelligence; whereas for men, interruptive behavior was key.

The takeaway is that the impact of hierarchy, which is often necessary for coordination, might hinge on both the level of hierarchical stability and the extent to which the group norms lead members to expect more cooperative or competitive patterns of engagement.

A team’s structure and composition are two foundational team-design levers available to cultivate team performance and overall collective intelligence, and this study suggests important nuances worth recognizing with respect to a team’s hierarchy *stability* and how that is related to the behavior that might be expected (or not) given the team’s composition. Particularly in dynamic settings in which diverse team members might not have much experience working together, leaders aiming to foster harmonious inclusivity and collaboration—not to mention better team performance—would thus be wise to help teams set precise norms and expectations about how to work together in order to foster collective attention and intelligence.

Access the full research paper here:

[*Collective Attention and Collective Intelligence: The Role of Hierarchy and Team Gender Composition*, Organization Science, 2023.](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/orsc.2022.1602)

Anita Williams Woolley, Rosalind M. Chow, Anna T. Mayo, Christoph Riedl, Jin Wook Chang.

<https://doi.org/10.1287/orsc.2022.1602>

[The Center for Innovative Leadership](https://carey.jhu.edu/cil) (CIL) at the Carey Business School aims to advance knowledge and build capacity for innovative leadership in modern organizations. CIL is a hub for new ideas and insights on leadership, combining faculty-led research, student-facing programming, and community-focused impact.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Gender diversity enhances communication styles, leading to improved collaboration and stability in teams, as diverse perspectives contribute to enhanced problem-solving and decision-making capabilities.

Mixed-gender teams exhibit higher collective intelligence, leveraging varied viewpoints for superior decision-making outcomes, thereby outperforming homogeneous teams in both efficiency and financial results.

Diverse leadership fosters inclusive environments, encouraging participation from all members, which enhances collective attention and team cohesion, ultimately driving team performance and innovation.
Gender-diverse teams are more innovative, as they generate a wider array of ideas and solutions, resulting in higher employee engagement and job satisfaction through inclusive participation.
